title-group.article-title: Nanotherapeutics for enhanced treatments for ovarian cancer: a comprehensive minireview

abstract.title: ABSTRACT
abstract.p: As one of the most lethal gynecological malignancies, ovarian cancer (OC) significantly impacts the health and quality of life of women globally. Currently, surgical intervention and chemotherapy remain the primary clinical treatment modalities for OC. With ongoing advancements in the understanding of molecular mechanisms, various therapeutic strategies such as poly-ADP-ribose polymerase inhibitors (PARPi) treatment, immunotherapy, and combination therapies have also been introduced into clinical practice. However, significant challenges, such as systemic adverse effects, high recurrence rates, and the development of drug resistance, greatly limit their efficacy. To overcome these limitations, nanotherapeutics have emerged as promising multifunctional drug delivery systems for both the diagnosis and treatment of OC over the past few decades, owing to their ability to specifically target malignant tissues and enable controlled drug release. In this paper, we first review the tumor-targeting mechanisms of nanoparticles, which can guide researchers in designing suitable and effective nanomedicines with translational potential. We subsequently provide a detailed overview of several representative nanotherapeutic approaches used in the treatment of OC, including their roles in enhancing chemotherapy, PARPi therapy, immunotherapy, light- or ultrasound-mediated therapies, and various combination strategies. Finally, we discuss future perspectives and potential directions for nanotherapeutics in advancing personalized and targeted treatment of OC.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: The latest global cancer data report for 2022 revealed that approximately 9.658 million new cancer cases and 4.309 million cancer-related deaths occurred among women worldwide (Bray et al. 2024; Siegel et al. 2025). Ovarian cancer (OC), one of the most lethal gynecological malignancies in women, accounting for 324,000 new cases globally, representing 3.3% of all female cancer diagnoses and ranking 8th in incidence. OC also caused 207,000 deaths, accounting for 4.8% of all female cancer mortalities and ranking 9th, significantly threatening the lives and well-being of women (Webb and Jordan 2024).

OC is highly invasive, with 80% of cases diagnosed at advanced stages of the pathology (stages III and IV), leading to a high recurrence rate after initial treatment (Ding et al. 2025). Currently, surgery and chemotherapy are the primary clinical treatments for OC. However, their effectiveness is often limited. For example, incomplete surgical resection can easily result in metastasis and recurrence. Non-specific chemotherapy may cause severe adverse effects in major organs, such as the heart, liver, and kidneys (McFadden et al. 2021). Moreover, the chemotherapy efficacy has been demonstrated to be significantly influenced by drug resistance and the complex tumor microenvironment.

In recent years, nanotechnology-based drug delivery platforms have become promising tools for enhancing the therapeutic efficacy of tumor therapy (Gulia et al. 2023). Nanocarriers significantly improve the tissue distribution and pharmacokinetics profiles of hydrophobic drugs while simultaneously reducing the systemic toxicity and adverse effects associated with conventional chemotherapeutic agents (Barani et al. 2021). In this review, we aim to outline current clinical approaches and, in particular, highlight the latest advancements in nanomedicines for the treatment of OC. In addition, we discuss various mechanisms underlying cellular internalization, targeted and stimuli-responsive drug release, as well as the effectiveness of combination therapies involving nanomedicines, which are influenced by their unique physicochemical properties. Ultimately, we summarize the merits and demerits of different types of nanomedicine-mediated cancer therapies, providing valuable insights for future research, development, and clinical translation of novel therapeutic agents.

sec.title: Current clinical treatment strategies for ovarian cancer
sec.p: Surgery is the main clinical strategy for treating OC, usually combined with chemotherapy to eradicate cancer cells. Currently, systemic platinum-based chemotherapy remains the first-line treatment for OC. While high-grade serous OC often demonstrates sensitivity to chemotherapy and most patients achieve clinical remission, approximately 80% will still experience recurrence following these treatments (Davis et al. 2014). Therefore, for patients with platinum-resistant OC, the standard treatment regimen involves the intravenous (i.v.) administration of carboplatin and paclitaxel every three weeks. Moreover, platinum-based agents can also be combined with doxorubicin, capecitabine, gemcitabine, or cyclophosphamide to improve the treatment efficacy. For instance, in patients with recurrent platinum-sensitive OC, adding gemcitabine to carboplatin significantly improved the progression-free survival, with an increase from 5.8 to 8.6 months (p = 0.003) compared to carboplatin alone (Pfisterer et al. 2006). The progress of clinical trials of various chemotherapy drug combinations is listed in Table 1.

caption.p: Clinical trials of typical chemotherapy in OC treatment.

tr.td: Gemcitabine plus carboplatin compared to carboplatin (progression-free survival (PFS): 8.6 vs. 5.8 months, p = 0.003)
Patients with histologically proven ovarian cancer with recurrence more than 6 months after first- or second-line platinum and taxane-based therapies
Pegylated liposomal doxorubicin and carboplatin compared to paclitaxel and carboplatin (PFS: 11.3 vs. 9.4 months, p = 0.005)
Platinum-sensitive recurrent epithelial ovarian, primary peritoneal, or fallopian tube cancer
Carboplatin and gemcitabine +/−bevacizumab (median PFS: 12.4 vs. 8.4 months, p < 0.0001)

Aged ≥ 18 years, had histologically confirmed epithelial ovarian, primary peritoneal, or fallopian tube carcinoma with first disease recurrence more than 6 months after first-line platinum-based chemotherapy, and an Eastern Cooperative Oncology Group performance status of 0−2
Carboplatin and pegylated liposomal doxorubicin and bevacizumab compared to carboplatin and gemcitabine and bevacizumab (median PFS: 13.3 vs. 11.6 months, p = 0.012)

sec.p: In light of the resurgence of ovarian cancer following initial therapy, researchers have developed poly-ADP-ribose polymerase (PARP) inhibitors to address the situation at the molecular level. Numerous studies have identified significant changes in key genes involved in DNA damage repair pathways during the progression of ovarian cancer. Notably, mutations in the breast cancer genes BRCA1 and BRCA2, which are crucial in the repair process of DNA double-strand breaks, account for approximately 25% of cases. Dysregulation of BRCA1 and BRCA2 expression is strongly associated with sensitivity to PARP inhibitors in OC, which helps to impede the repair pathway of DNA single-strand breaks, leading to cancer cells' apoptosis. Clinical evidence supports the notion that in OC patients receiving PARP inhibitor treatments, aberrations in the BRCA status can serve as predictive markers for increased overall survival (OS) and progression-free survival (PFS). The established treatment protocol for OC patients with somatic or germline BRCA mutations involves maintenance therapy utilizing PARP inhibitors following first-line platinum-based chemotherapy. Furthermore, the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) has approved the PARP inhibitor olaparib for the treatment of advanced OC patients harboring germline or advanced BRCA mutations. Additionally, several studies have suggested that inhibitors targeting cell cycle regulation or DNA damage repair mechanisms, such as checkpoint kinase 1 inhibitors and ataxia telangiectasia and Rad3-related protein inhibitors, exhibit effects comparable to those of PARP inhibitors. Further details on contemporary treatment modalities for OC are delineated in Table 2.

caption.p: Clinical trials of typical PARPi in OC treatment.

tr.td: Newly diagnosed stage III or IV high-grade serous or endometrioid ovarian cancer with a BRCA mutation
Olaparib compared to placebo (hazard ratio (HR) 0.55, 95% confidence interval (CI): 0.4−0.76; median overall survival (OS) was not reached vs. 75 months, p = 0.0004)
Homologous recombination deficiency (HRD), advanced high-grade serous or endometrioid ovarian cancer
Niraparib compared to placebo (median PFS: 21.9 vs. 10.4 months, 95% CI: 0.31−0.59; p < 0.001)
Newly diagnosed advanced ovarian cancer after response to platinum chemotherapy
Rucaparib compared to placebo (median PFS: 28.7 vs. 11.3 months, p = 0.0004)
Olaparib compared to placebo (PFS: 19.1 vs. 5.5 months, p < 0.0001)
Recurrent, platinum-sensitive, germline BRCA (gBRCA) and gBRCA Wild Type (WT)
Niraparib compared to placebo (gBRCA: PFS: 21.0 vs. 5.5 months, p < 0.001; HRD/non-gBRCA: PFS: 12.9 vs. 3.8 months, p < 0.001; Non-gBRCA: PFS: 9.3 vs. 3.9 months, p < 0.001)
Rucaparib compared to placebo (BRCAm: PFS:16.6 vs. 5.4 months, p < 0.0001; Intention-to-treat (ITT): PFS:10.8 vs. 5.4 months, p < 0.0001; HRD: PFS:13.6 vs. 5.4 months, p < 0.0001)

High-grade serous ovarian carcinoma; Cohorts 1−3: platinum-resistant disease; Cohort 1: BRCA negative with 3 or more chemotherapy lines; Cohort 2: BRCA negative with fewer than 3 chemotherapy lines; Cohort 3: BRCA positive with prior PARP therapy; Cohort 4: platinum-refractory disease
Prexasertib administered IV compared to placebo (The objective response rate (ORR) in platinum-resistant patients (Cohorts 1−3) was 12.1%, and 6.9% in platinum-refractory patients)

sec.p: Since the recognition of immunotherapy, particularly immune checkpoint blockade (ICB), by the awarding of the 2018 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine, its application in the clinical treatment of cancer has become widespread. Various immunotherapies, including ICB, adoptive cell therapy (ACT), cancer vaccines, and cytokines, have been approved for cancer treatment. However, the clinical advancements in OC immunotherapy are not satisfactory. OC remains one of the few cancers for which the FDA has not approved any treatment indications. This is primarily attributed to the low immunogenicity and immunosuppressive microenvironment of OC, which is characterized by a high presence of immunosuppressive cells, including CD4+ regulatory T cells (Tregs), M2-type tumor-associated macrophages (TAMs), cancer-associated fibroblasts (CAFs), and myeloid-derived suppressor cells (MDSCs), all of which hinder robust immune responses. Additionally, the clinical data demonstrate that nivolumab alone did not improve OS (primary endpoint) and exhibited less satisfactory PFS results when compared to chemotherapy in patients with platinum-resistant OC. Consequently, combination therapies that incorporate chemotherapy, PARP inhibitors, epigenetic agents, anti-angiogenic agents, and antibody-drug conjugates (ADCs) have become promising methods to increase the effectiveness of immunotherapy in advanced ovarian cancer. More information about related treatment strategies for OC is shown in Table 3.

caption.p: Clinical trials of typical immunotherapy in OC treatment.

tr.td: Pembrolizumab ORR: 7.4% (received 1−3 prior lines), 9.9% (received 4-6 prior lines), disease control rate (DCR): 37.2% (received 1-3 prior lines), 37.4% (received 4-6 prior lines)
Triple-negative breast cancer or ovarian cancer
Recurrent platinum-resistant ovarian, fallopian tube or peritoneal cancer
Pembrolizumab + Pegylated liposomal doxorubicin (Clinical benefit rate: 52.2%, ORR: 26.1%)
Nivolumab compare to chemotherapy (gemcitabine or pegylated liposomal doxorubicin)(Nivolumab did not improve OS (primary endpoint) and showed worse PFS than chemotherapy in platinum-resistant OC)
Ovarian cancer patients in first or second remission
MUC1-targeted dendritic cell vaccine PFS: 13 months (first clinical remission), > 42 months (second clinical remission)
Multiepitope FRa vaccine + durvalumab (Stable disease (SD): 33.3%, partial response (PR): 3.7%)
Tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes + lymphodepleting chemotherapy (cyclophosphamide and fludarabine) + IL-2 + Ipilimumab + Nivolumab (12-month SD: 83.3%, PR: 16.7%)
Metastatic pancreatic cancer, serous epithelial ovarian cancer, or pleural mesothelioma
IFN-γ + cisplatin + cyclophosphamide (Complete response (CR): 68%, 3-year progression-free rate (PFR): 51%, 3-year OS: 74%)

sec.title: Nanotherapeutics for ovarian cancer therapy
sec.p: Nanotherapeutics have remained versatile delivery system for therapeutic and diagnostic agents in recent decades, as they can protect the payload from degradation and increase their bioavailability, while being transported through blood vessels to access malignant tissues. In addition, various functional nanoparticles have been developed by modifying with targeting ligands and surface chemistries to reduce toxicity to normal tissues and improve the precision of therapeutics by specifically responding to the unique tumor microenvironment (Sun et al. 2017; Zhou et al. 2023; Mai et al. 2023; An et al. 2024).

Despite showing significant treatment efficiency for animal solid tumors of anti-cancer nanomedicines, limited formulations have been approved for clinical use in tumor therapy by the FDA and the European Medicines Agency. For example, the nanoformulations such as Doxil, Abraxane, and Vyxeos have been applied in the treatment of pancreatic adenocarcinoma, breast cancer, and leukemia with reduced systemic toxicity. Regrettably, however, the clinical outcomes are unsatisfactory when compared to standard chemotherapy, with no significant elevation in the objective response rate (ORR) and PFS (Zhou et al. 2020; Pallares et al. 2025). Therefore, understanding the inherent mechanisms of nanomedicine delivery to clinical human tumors is of great significance to enhance the therapeutic effects of nanomedicines. Currently, the passive and active transport processes are regarded as the mainstream mechanisms.

sec.title: Passive transport process of nanoparticle delivery
sec.p: Since the broughtup of the enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) effect in 1986, the nanoparticles have been deemed to passively enter the tumors via gaps between abnormal leaky vasculature and to be retained by impaired lymphatic drainage. Previous immunohistochemistry and electron microscopy studies of the blood vasculature in murine mammary carcinomas have showed that 14% of the blood vessel surface is lined by poorly connected cells along with endothelium intercellular openings of size ranging from 0.3 to 4.7 µm while transcellular holes of sizes ranging from 0.2 to 0.9 µm (Nguyen et al. 2024; Belyaev et al. 2025). Thus, current nanomedicines are designed with ideal sizes of 10–200 nm to realize passive accumulation in solid tumors, which relies mainly on the EPR effect (Figure 1a). However, human tumors exhibited more heterogeneous and diverse genetic patterns, possessing non-uniform vascularization, vessel fenestration and parenchymal microenvironment compared to animal models, leading to unsatisfactory treatment outcomes (Ding et al. 2020). Moreover, the dense perivascular layers near the blood vessels as well as the high-density matrix, complex components, and high interstitial fluid pressure in the tumor microenvironment strongly impede the permeation and diffusion of nanoparticles. All these variable factors severely compromise the distribution and clinical therapeutic effects of nanomedicines within solid tumors (Von Roemeling et al. 2017). In particular, Warren Chan’s group has demonstrated that only an average of 0.7% of nanoparticles of intravenously injection reach solid tumors, indicating that the number of endothelial lining gaps is too limited to accomplish efficient delivery of nanoparticles, allowing researchers to reevaluate the reliance of drug delivery on the EPR effect (Sindhwani et al. 2020).

caption.p: The passive and active transport mechanisms of nanoparticle delivery. (a) The passive delivery of nanoparticles is based on the enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) effects, which states that nanoparticles infiltrate tumors through vascular gaps and are unable to exit due to the collapse of tumor lymphatics. (b) The active delivery of nanoparticles is based on the active transport and retention (ATR) principle, which postulates that nanoparticles enter the tumor through cell-mediated transportation, including transcytosis pathway from the entry site to the exit site (Nguyen et al. 2024). Copyright 2024, Springer Nature.

sec.title: Active transport process of nanoparticle delivery
sec.p: As evidenced by Warren Chan’s study, approximately 97% of nanoparticles can be transported by endothelial cells via an active transcytosis process in specific tumor models, such as U87-MG glioblastoma and 4T1 breast cancer models, driving investigation into the reasons behind this phenomenon (Sindhwani et al. 2020). The intravital microscopy tracking images also have indicated that the nanoparticles can be actively delivered by endothelial and immune cells in overcoming blood barriers and retained in the tumor tissue because of the interactions with tumor components and exit the tumor via lymphatic vessels, which this mechanism is defined as the ATR principle (Figure 1b) (Sindhwani et al. 2020; Lin et al. 2023). In contrast to the passive transport via the EPR effect, the active delivery process, including transendothelial delivery and cell-mediated delivery based on the ATR principle, involves an energy-dependent process to migrate nanoparticles from the blood vessel into deep tumor regions (Author 2020).

As for the transendothelial permeation of nanoparticles, a specialized phenotype of endothelial cells in tumor vessels, known as nanoparticle transport endothelial cells (N-TECs), plays an important role in the transcytosis process, which involves the active uptake of nanoparticles at the apical side, followed by intracellular transport and their eventual release into the tumor interstitium through exocytosis or diffusion pathways (Kingston et al. 2021). As an important component of the transcytosis process, the spatial localization of N-TECs across the ovarian cancer vasculature determines the entry points and subsequent distribution of nanoparticles. This spatial heterogeneity results in variable delivery efficiency, potentially leaving tumor regions distant from these active transport sites inaccessible to nanotherapeutics. These active processes necessitate structural changes in endothelial cells and encompass mechanisms such as transcytosis, vesiculo-vacuolar organelles, and the influence of migrating cells. The pathways involved in transcytosis comprise various mechanisms, including phagocytosis for larger particles, cellular debris, and microorganisms, as well as micropinocytosis for smaller particles ranging from a few to several hundred nanometers in size. Micropinocytosis can be further categorized into caveolae-mediated endocytosis, clathrin-mediated endocytosis, clathrin-independent endocytosis, and caveolae-independent endocytosis (Pandit et al. 2020; Zhou et al. 2022). Typically, nanomedicines exploit receptor-mediated transcytosis (RMT) and adsorptive-mediated transcytosis (AMT) to facilitate effective tumor extravasation (Figure 2). Specific interactions with receptors on the cell surface, such as caveolae glycoprotein receptors (gp60 and gp90), transferrin receptors, or leptin receptors, promote nanoparticle internalization via endocytosis followed by subsequent intracellular release of therapeutic cargo (Song et al. 2018; Yang et al. 2019; Zhang et al. 2020). Schnitzer et al. demonstrated that receptor binding to glycoproteins within caveolae leads to caveolae-mediated endocytosis and the active transport of blood albumins across vascular endothelial barriers through transcytotic mechanisms. Additionally, it has been reported that ICAM-1 modulation of vascular endothelial cells along with platelet factor 4 in injured blood vessels enhances targeted nanoparticle transcytosis (Manthe et al. 2020; Tylawsky et al. 2023; Belyaev et al. 2025). However, RMT-based nanomedicines have only exhibited moderately enhanced efficacy. In contrast, AMT-based nanomedicines have shown significantly improved effectiveness. Positively charged TAT peptides enable nanoparticles to undergo active intracellular transport along microtubule tracks before being released through exocytotic processes or vesicle shedding (Jin et al. 2013; Liu et al. 2017). Recent research conducted by Shen’s group revealed that a charge reversal process can initiate caveolae-mediated endocytosis and transcytotic activity of polymer-drug conjugates; this facilitates both transendothelial and transcellular transport while promoting distribution throughout solid tumors (Zhou et al. 2019; Sun et al. 2023; Wang et al. 2023). They further developed a series of polyzwitterion-drug conjugates capable of adsorbing onto tumor endothelial cells, thereby facilitating their extravasation into the tumor stroma and subsequent infiltration through endocytosis-mediated processes. This approach enables efficient tumor treatment (Chen et al. 2021; Yuan et al. 2024; Zhao et al. 2025; Li et al. 2025). The correlations among the structure, transcytosis, extravasation, and efficacy of transcytotic nanomedicines are continuously being explored to inform their rational design for clinical translation.

caption.p: The transcytosis mechanisms including receptor-mediated transcytosis (RMT) and adsorptive-mediated transcytosis (AMT) of nanoparticle delivery into tumors (Pandit et al. 2020). Copyright 2020, Springer Nature.

sec.p: In addition to the aforementioned transcytosis pathway for actively crossing endothelial barriers, cell-mediated transcytosis has also been investigated. Various types of blood-circulating cells, including neutrophils, lymphocytes, and monocytes, can enhance nanoparticle delivery to tumors owing to their natural mechanisms for traversing endothelial barriers. This can occur either by anchoring nanoparticles to their surface or via internal uptake, making these cells promising vehicles for delivering therapeutic agents into malignant tissues (Pandit et al. 2020; Luo et al. 2023; Yuan and Hu 2024; Feng et al. 2024). The cell-mediated ‘hitchhiking’ of nanoparticles can be achieved through ex vivo loading but may also take place through in situ interactions within the bloodstream, influencing the delivery dynamics of non-stealth nanomaterials. It has been reported that perivascular TAMs can sequester nanoparticles adjacent to blood vessels and transport them five times deeper into tumors compared to passive diffusion alone (Lin et al. 2022). Circulating leukocytes and marginated neutrophils typically uptake a portion of intravenously injected nanomaterials and can significantly alter their biodistribution patterns. Furthermore, leukocyte trafficking induces leakiness in vascular structures due to local thermal ablation or nitric oxide exposure on the vascular basement membrane; this phenomenon allows nanoparticles to access tumors via this route (Wang et al. 2024; Jiang et al. 2025).

While these studies highlighted the potential significance of active transcellular transport over passive paracellular leakage, direct and quantitative evidence establishing its role in ovarian cancer remains sparse. The heterogeneity of vascular phenotypes across cancer types necessitates dedicated investigations to determine whether and to what extent this mechanism operates in ovarian tumors. Therefore, elucidating the transcytosis efficiency and its regulatory mechanisms in ovarian cancer represents a critical frontier for enhancing the delivery of nanotherapeutics. In addition, the relationship between nanoparticle design and the ATR mechanisms needs to be further investigated (Nuhn 2023).

sec.title: Nanomedicines used for platinum-based chemotherapy
sec.p: The first-line chemotherapy drugs for OC mainly consist of platinum-based chemotherapeutics, which primarily induce significant DNA damage and trigger cell apoptosis by forming irreparable platinum-DNA adducts. Although significant progress has been made in their clinical application, severe systemic toxicities such as ototoxicity, neurotoxicity, and nephrotoxicity, as well as drug resistance caused by platinum-based drugs, often lead to poor patient compliance and treatment efficacy. To address these challenges, various nanomaterials have been developed for the targeted delivery of platinum-based drugs aimed at minimizing side effects and enhancing therapeutic outcomes. For example, Shariati et al. developed cross-linked cisplatin-loaded polyarginine-hyaluronic acid nanoparticles (Cis-pARG-HA NPs) possessing high colloidal stability and tumor-targeting ability, with the aim of treating disseminated advanced ovarian tumors (Figure 3a) (Shariati et al. 2020). The efficacy of Cis-pARG-HA NPs was further enhanced by the use of pressurized intraperitoneal aerosol chemotherapy (PIPAC). This technique ensures a more homogeneous distribution and deeper penetration of nanoparticles into peritoneal tumors, as confirmed by the observation of increased fluorescent NP deposition even in areas distant from the aerosol nozzle. Notably, intraperitoneal (IP) chemotherapy is also a standard administration method for treating ovarian cancer. To increase the efficacy of IP chemotherapy, nanonization strategies have been developed to improve drug delivery and reduce systemic toxicity. For instance, RGD-targeted calcium phosphate nanoparticles (RGD-CaPO/DOX) were designed to co-deliver doxorubicin and calcium ions, leveraging redox and calcium dyshomeostasis to induce synergistic apoptosis in tumor cells, thereby effectively inhibiting the progression of intraperitoneal disseminated tumors (Qiu et al. 2022). Alternatively, a cathepsin B-activated doxorubicin prodrug nanoformulation (PNPs) was developed for IP delivery. This system ensures tumor-specific drug release, prolongs peritoneal retention, and enhances tumor penetration through both direct diffusion and vascular-mediated pathways, demonstrating superior efficacy and reduced toxicity in patient-derived xenograft models. (Kim et al. 2021) Zhang et al. developed platinum nanoclusters (Pt NCs) encapsulated in polyethylenimine (PEI) that can induce autophagy by inhibiting the PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway, showing promising chemotherapeutic effects in patients with cisplatin-resistant OC (Figure 3b). (Zhang et al. 2022) In addition, platinum (IV) prodrugs with the potential to reduce side effects have also been modified to improve the therapeutic efficacy of platinum-based drugs. For instance, platinum prodrugs can combine with functional substances such as gossypol, isoliquiritigenin, tolfenamic acid, to form nano-prodrug systems, achieving highly effective synergistic anti-tumor effects against ovarian cancer by mechanisms including strong binding to apoptosis-related proteins such as Bcl-2, Bcl-xL, and Mcl-1, as well as inhibition of tumor cell growth during the G2/M phase and suppression of COX-2 expression (Figure 3c) (Li et al. 2024).

caption.p: The platinum-based nanomedicines for ovarian cancer chemotherapy (Shariati et al. 2020; Zhang et al. 2022; Li et al. 2024). Copyright 2020, American Chemical Society. Copyright 2022, American Chemical Society. Copyright 2024, Wiley-VCH.

sec.p: However, severe drug resistance still poses a great challenge in the clinical application of platinum-based drugs. Research has shown that the upregulation of ATP-binding cassette (ABC) family members and the vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) signaling pathway enable cancer cells to reactivate and proliferate, leading to significant resistance to platinum-based drugs. To address this, Liu et al. constructed selenium nanoparticles (SeNPs) loaded with lentinan (LNT), which effectively inhibits the expression of tumor ABC transporters and VEGF by activating DNA damage and destroying the mitochondrial membrane potential (Liu et al. 2025). This approach helps to maintain good chemotherapeutic efficacy during the drug-free intervals of cisplatin treatment. Another study found that BTB and CNC homology 1 (BACH1) are overexpressed in cisplatin (DDP)-resistant OC tissues and upregulates the ‘don't eat me’ signal CD47. Based on this, Gong et al. developed a pH-responsive nanomedicine (H/D@FA-CaP-NPs), in which folic acid (FA) facilitates targeting of OC cells, while the hemin chloride inhibits BACH 1 and downregulates CD47, thereby promoting tumor cell apoptosis and inducing macrophage phagocytosis of tumors (Gong et al. 2024). Wang et al. employed synthetic low-cholesterol HDL-like nanoparticles (HDL NPs) to block SR-B1-mediated cholesterol uptake, thereby inducing the death of drug-resistant ovarian cancer cells and suppressing tumor growth (Wang et al. 2024). Additionally, Liu et al. found that antagonizing the cGAS-STING signaling in CAFs can also significantly increase the responsiveness of OC to platinum-based chemotherapy (Liu et al. 2024). As more drug resistance mechanisms are identified, newly designed nanodrug systems will provide effective approaches to combat the challenge of chemotherapy resistance.

sec.title: Nanomedicines used for PARP inhibitor treatments
sec.p: Poly-ADP-ribose polymerase inhibitors (PARPi) were first approved by the FDA in 2018 for the maintenance treatment of patients with platinum-sensitive recurrent and advanced ovarian cancer. PARP plays a crucial role in single-strand DNA break (SSB) repair; thus, its inhibition causes the accumulation of DNA damage. However, given their limited efficacy and potential side effects, prolonged use of PARPi may even cause hematological diseases in advanced cancer patients. Therefore, many studies have focused on the use of nanodrug delivery systems to enhance the therapeutic effects and reduce side effects. For example, Wang et al. designed a tumor-targeting peptide TMTP1-modified mesoporous polydopamine (MPDA)-based nanomedicines (TPNPs) (Wang et al. 2023). The nanoformulation significantly improved the pharmacokinetic profile of olaparib. Specifically, the elimination half-life was extended to 2.07 h, approximately 1.9-fold longer than that of the free drug (1.08 h). The area under the concentration‒time curve (AUC) also markedly increased by approximately 1.7-fold (from 3.83 to 6.63 mg/L·h), while total body clearance (CL) was significantly reduced. These quantitative results clearly define the key pharmacokinetic advantages of the nanoformulation, including prolonged systemic exposure and enhanced drug retention, which underlie its improved therapeutic efficacy. TPNPs induced abundant OC cells apoptosis and significantly improved therapeutic efficacy when combined with adavosertib (Ada) and olaparib (Ola) in murine models (Figure 4a). The HRD status and BRCA mutations are critical biomarkers that can strategically guide the selection and application of nanomedicines, particularly in the context of targeted therapies such as PARPi. Tumors exhibiting HRD or BRCA1/2 mutations possess inherent deficiencies in DNA damage repair pathways. This vulnerability creates a strong therapeutic rationale for deploying nanoformulated PARPi, as nanocarriers can enhance drug delivery to tumor sites while minimizing systemic exposure. Lin et al identified the compound SN38, a camptothecin (CPT) analog, which can strongly sensitize BRCA-positive OC cells to a PARP inhibitor by impeding homologous recombination (HR) repair functions (Figure 4b). (Lin et al. 2022) Kaplan et al. reported that cediranib enhances susceptibility to olaparib by inhibiting the expression of the homologous DNA repair factors BRCA1/2 and RAD51 (Kaplan et al. 2019). Therefore, the combination of cediranib and olaparib can strongly block the DNA repair mechanisms of solid tumors, leading to strong synthetic lethality. In addition, other inhibitors that target DNA repair pathways have been developed to synergize with PARPi to improve their efficacy. For example, Tao et al. reported that the inhibition of lysine-specific demethylase 1 (LSD1) blocks HR repair by directly inhibiting the expression of BRCA1/2 and RAD51 (Figure 4c) (Tao et al. 2023). The combination with LSD1 inhibitors can significantly expand the application of PARPi in patients with HR-positive tumors. Ravindranathan et al. reported that the deletion or inhibition of poly (ADP-β-ribose) glycohydrolase (PARG) induces strong replication stress, increasing the sensitivity of OC tumor cells to DNA-damaging agents (Ravindranathan et al. 2024).

caption.p: The PARP inhibitor-based nanomedicines for ovarian cancer chemotherapy (Lin et al. 2022; Wang et al. 2023; Tao et al. 2023). Copyright 2022, Elsevier. Copyright 2022, Wiley-VCH. Copyright 2023, Springer Nature.

sec.p: However, with the rising utility of PARPi, addressing the issue of drug resistance has become an urgent priority. Wu et al compared the differentially expressed genes between platinum-sensitive and platinum-resistant groups in the TCGA OC cohort and reported that, in the TCGA-OV cohort, lncRNA PART1 expression was significantly higher in platinum-sensitive patients than in platinum-resistant patients (Wu et al. 2024). These findings suggest that the lncRNA PART1 may represent a new target for reversing PARPi resistance and improving the prognosis of OC. Moreover, Zhan et al. demonstrated that deleting KAT6A in PARP-resistant ovarian cancer cells restored their sensitivity to PARPi (Zhan et al. 2024). In these resistant cells, KAT6A undergoes liquid‒liquid phase separation (LLPS) dependent on APEX1, releasing trapped PARPi from chromatin and thereby revealing a potential clinical treatment strategy for PARPi-resistant OC. Therefore, given the exploration of the above mechanism, the development of novel nanomaterials and technologies is anticipated to address the issue of PARPi resistance.

sec.title: Nanomedicines used for immunotherapy
sec.p: The low immunogenicity and suppressive immune microenvironment in OC severely limit the therapeutic responses to various immunotherapies. The precise activation of the cGAS-STING pathway is crucial for converting immunologically ‘cold’ ovarian tumors into ‘hot’ ones. To increase the immunogenicity of tumor cells, Ao et al. designed a responsive self-assembled nanoparticle (NP 2) constructed from a glutathione (GSH)-sensitive doxorubicin prodrug (PHHM-SS-DOX) and a DNA-PKcs inhibitor (Ao et al. 2025). NP 2 released doxorubicin (DOX) and a DNA-PKcs inhibitor at tumor sites, inducing apoptosis while inhibiting the non-homologous end joining (NHEJ) pathway (Figure 5a). This treatment exacerbates DNA damage and immunogenicity, further activating the cGAS-STING immune pathway. The magnitude and duration of cGAS-STING signaling are directly proportional to the amount of cytosolic dsDNA. The nanoparticle's controlled release mechanism maintains effective intracellular drug levels, resulting in a stronger and more prolonged immune activation compared to free drugs. Ultimately, this reprograms the immune microenvironment into a ‘hot immune’ OC tumor, significantly increasing the response to immune checkpoint inhibitors (ICIs). Sandoval et al. reported that the FDA-approved iron chelator deferiprone can reprogram OC cells into an immunostimulatory state by inducing cellular DNA damage and immunogenicity generation (Sandoval et al. 2024). This process leads to the overexpression of molecules that produce type I interferon (IFN) and activate natural killer (NK) cells. When combined with chemotherapy, these treatments significantly prolong the survival of mice with ovarian cancer, suggesting an alternative immunotherapy strategy. To improve the immunosuppressive microenvironment, Chen et al. engineered calcium alendronate (CaALN) nanoparticles to facilitate the sustained expression of HER2 and CD3 (Figure 5b). (Chen et al. 2023) This innovation, simulated by a bispecific antibody, promotes polyclonal T cell activation and delays the progression of intraperitoneal xenograft OC tumor models. In another study, Li et al. reported a local peritoneal hydrogel that incorporates Siglec-10-engineered M1-type macrophage exosomes and an efferocytosis inhibitor (Figure 5c). (Li et al. 2023) Following X-ray radiation to induce immunogenicity, this hydrogel regulates the polarization and efferocytosis of peritoneal macrophages. As a result, it enhances the phagocytosis of tumor cells and activates antigen presentation, resulting in an effective treatment of ovarian cancer in mice and prevention of recurrence. Hua et al designed a responsive supramolecular hydrogel (Gel Nap-S + HG) constructed by Nap-Phe-Phe-Glu-Glu-LeuTyr-Arg-Thr-Gln-Ser-Ser-Ser-Asn-Leu-OH (Nap-S) loaded with the SIK2 inhibitor HG-9-91-01 (HG) (Hua et al. 2022). When SIK2 is overexpressed in OC cells, HG can be released, thereby inhibiting adipocyte-mediated ovarian cancer cell proliferation and metastasis and enhancing the suppression of murine ovarian tumor growth and metastasis. In summary, nanomedicines offer an effective approach for immunotherapy or combination therapy in ovarian cancer.

caption.p: The immunotherapy used for ovarian cancer treatments (Chen et al. 2023; Li et al. 2023; Ao et al. 2025). Copyright 2025, Elsevier. Copyright 2023, Wiley-VCH. Copyright 2023, American Chemical Society.

sec.title: Other cancer treatment strategies
sec.p: The tissue and microenvironmental heterogeneity of OC severely restricts the effectiveness of traditional treatment strategies. Anti-tumor approaches that utilize external regulation methods, such as light and ultrasound, possess several advantages, including high controllability, non-invasiveness, and the ability to penetrate deep tissues, providing new opportunities for targeted therapy in OC. For example, nanoplatforms developed from inorganic materials such as Fe3O4, gold nanoparticles, and organic materials such as conjugated polymers can generate promising photothermal effects when exposed to light, causing tumor necrosis and antigen release, which is beneficial for enhancing the anti-tumor immunotherapy (Figure 6a) (Xiong et al. 2021; Yu et al. 2022; Shen et al. 2022; Li et al. 2024). Except that, photodynamic therapy and sonodynamic therapy are increasingly used to target solid tumors by generating large amounts of reactive oxygen species (ROS) that can kill cancer cells. Various photosensitizers and sonosensitizers, such as pyropheophorbide-a, Ce6, TiO2, 4,4’,4’’,4’’’-(porphine-5,10,15,20-tetrayl)tetrakis(benzoic acid), have been employed to facilitate tumor immunogenic cell death, converting ‘immune-cold tumors’ into ‘immune-hot tumors’ and thus improving the antitumor efficacy of photodynamic or sonodynamic therapy combined with immunotherapy (Figure 6b). (Lee et al. 2021; Huang et al. 2024; Boidin et al. 2024; Xie et al. 2025) Moreover, Chen et al. fabricated folate-targeted nanoparticles (FA-DCNP) loaded with 6-diazo-5-oxo-L-norleucine (DON) and calcium carbonate (CaCO3), which demonstrate a combined anti-tumor effect through the inhibition of glutamine metabolism as well as calcium ions overload (Chen et al. 2025). Luo et al designed hypoxia-responsive polymer nanoparticles for delivering the SCD1 inhibitor PluriSIn 1 (Plu) and the apoptosis inducer Auranofin (Aur), leading to ferroptosis in tumor cells and offering a new approach for treating refractory OC (Luo et al. 2025). Shi et al reported an amphiphilic dendrimer encapsulating imatinib (IM), which shows synergistic anti-tumor activity with cisplatin in a xenograft mouse model of metastatic OC by targeting CD117, which is overexpressed on ovarian cancer stem cells (CSC). (Shi et al. 2025) Beyond the well-characterized roles of ABC transporters and VEGF signaling, ovarian cancer employs unique molecular mechanisms to evade therapy, among which the transmembrane mucin MUC16 and its soluble form CA125 are of particular importance. MUC16 promotes chemoresistance by inducing epithelial-mesenchymal transition (EMT), enhancing tumor cell plasticity and stemness. Meanwhile, CA125 not only serves as a diagnostic biomarker but also promotes an immunosuppressive microenvironment that protects tumor cells. Targeting MUC16 has emerged as a promising strategy. Hu et al. designed an MUC16-targeted high-valent selenium (Se) nanomedicine (MUC16-SeMnf@Res) that induced OC cell apoptosis by inducing GSH depletion and excessive ROS production to disrupt the intracellular redox homeostasis (Figure 6c) (Hu et al. 2025). Hu et al. also designed a novel ionic drug capable of achieving pH/GSH dual-responsive ion release (Fe2+/Na+/S2O82−) that generates toxic sulfate (·SO4-) and hydroxyl (·OH) radicals to induce ferroptosis and pyroptosis in tumor cells (Figure 6d) (Hu et al. 2023). The tumor-responsive liquid nanoparticles hold significant guiding value for the development of ion-mediated disease therapy in the future.

caption.p: The novel nanomedicines designed for ovarian cancer treatments (Yu et al. 2022; Hu et al. 2023; Huang et al. 2024; Hu et al. 2025). Copyright 2021, Wiley-VCH. Copyright 2024, Wiley-VCH. Copyright 2025, Wiley-VCH. Copyright 2023, Wiley-VCH.

sec.title: Limitations and translational challenges of current nanotherapeutics
sec.p: Despite the considerable promise of nanotherapeutics in preclinical and clinical studies (Table 4), their clinical translation for OC therapy has faced significant hurdles, yielding only modest improvements in patient outcomes compared to conventional therapies. This disparity stems from several interconnected limitations that underscore the inadequacy of conventional design principles in addressing the complex human cancers.

caption.p: Clinical progress of nanoformulated therapeutics in OC.

sec.title: Inefficient tumor accumulation and penetration of nanotherapeutics
sec.p: The design of nanotherapeutics has long been guided by the EPR effect. However, the vascular heterogeneity and elevated interstitial fluid pressure in human tumors result in inconsistent and generally low levels of nanoparticle accumulation. While active targeting ligands (e.g. folate, transferrin) aim to improve specificity, their efficacy is frequently compromised by the heterogeneous expression of target receptors and the formation of protein corona that masks the targeting moieties in vivo (Wang et al. 2023). Although the introduction of ATR principle, encompassing transcytosis and cell-mediated transport, has shown promise in enhancing drug delivery in solid tumors such as hepatocellular carcinoma (Zhou et al. 2019), its potential remains largely untapped and requires further experimental validation and clinical investigation in ovarian cancer.

sec.title: Ovarian cancer-specific physiological and immunosuppressive barriers
sec.p: OC presents unique physical and biological barriers that severely limit nanotherapeutic efficacy. The dense fibrous stroma and high-density extracellular matrix characteristic of advanced OC create a formidable physical barrier, strongly impeding nanoparticle penetration and diffusion. Concurrently, the immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment (TME) plays dual role. For example, CAFs contribute to the dense stroma and sequester therapeutics, and TAMs can actively engulf and remove nanoparticles, diverting them from their intended target (Jiménez-Sánchez et al. 2017; Qiu et al. 2024; Madan et al. 2025).

sec.title: The high-dimensional challenge of rational design and scalability
sec.p: The transition from laboratory research to clinical application is hampered by fundamental design and manufacturing hurdles. The rational design of nanotherapeutics is a high-dimensional optimization problem, requiring the simultaneous balancing of conflicting parameters, such as size, surface charge, drug loading, stealth, and targeting ligand density, to achieve optimal in vivo performance. (Sun et al. 2017; Zhou et al. 2023). Traditional trial-and-error approaches are ill-suited to navigating this vast design space. Furthermore, the scalable and reproducible synthesis of complex, multi-functional nanotherapeutics under good manufacturing practice (GMP) standards remains challenging and costly. This creates a critical trade-off between the sophistication required for efficacy and the simplicity needed for robust manufacturing and clinical translation.

In summary, the limitations of current nanotherapeutics are not merely incremental but foundational. The overreliance on passive targeting, the failure to adequately address the specific physical and biological barriers of ovarian cancer, and the complexities of rational design and manufacturing collectively necessitate a paradigm shift. Overcoming these challenges requires a move away from universal nanotherapeutic design toward intelligent, data-driven, and patient-tailored strategies.

sec.title: Conclusions and outlook
sec.p: In conclusion, nanotherapeutics have established considerable promise as versatile platforms for advancing ovarian cancer treatment. The rational design of key physicochemical parameters enables nanotherapeutics to improve tumor-specific drug delivery, increase therapeutic efficacy, and reduce systemic toxicity across diverse modalities, including chemotherapy, PARP inhibition, and immunotherapy. However, the transition from promising preclinical data to broad clinical success remains challenging. The inherent limitations of passive targeting, combined with the dense stromal barrier and immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment of ovarian cancer, reveal the inadequacy of universal nanotherapeutic solutions. To overcome these translational barriers, the field must shift toward an intelligent and precision-engineered paradigm.
The next generation of nanotherapeutics will need to be adaptive, biologically informed, and patient-specific. Key directions expected to drive this evolution include:

list-item.p: Data-driven design through barrier analysis. Moving beyond generic carrier systems requires the integration of multi-omics analyses, such as proteomics, transcriptomics, and spatial genomics, with computational modeling (Finkernagel et al. 2019; Xiao et al. 2021; Qian et al. 2024; Denisenko et al. 2024; Breinig et al. 2025). This approach systematically identify dominant delivery barriers within patient subgroups, enabling the rational design of nanotherapeutics with customized functionalities (e.g. proteolytic sensitivity or immune-cell tropism) tailored to overcome patient-specific obstacles.

AI-guided multi-objective optimization. Designing nanotherapeutics that simultaneously satisfy multiple competing constraints, including size, circulation time, targeting capability, and release kinetics, represents a high-dimensional challenge. Leveraging AI-driven generative models and multi-objective optimization algorithms, inspired by recent advances in molecular drug design, can efficiently navigate this complex design space and accelerate the development of high-performance, translatable nanomedicines (Zhang et al. 2023; Wu et al. 2024; Wang et al. 2025; Zhuang et al. 2025; Chen et al. 2025).

Harnessing active transport and anatomical advantages. Intelligent design must also be extended to exploit biology itself. A deeper understanding and exploitation of active transport pathways, including transcytosis and cell-mediated hitchhiking, will be key to moving beyond passive accumulation. Coupling these strategies with anatomical considerations, such as the development of advanced intraperitoneal delivery platforms, can maximize drug exposure within the peritoneal cavity, where ovarian cancer predominantly progresses.

sec.p: By integrating data-rich biological insights with AI-powered design and innovative materials engineering, the future of ovarian cancer nanotherapeutics lies in creating adaptive, intelligent, and patient-tailored systems. This synergistic strategy holds the potential to unlock the full clinical promise of nanomedicines and meaningfully improve outcomes for patients.
